content.text: file at its root. It is the single source of truth for what your project is, what it depends on, and how to build and test it. Understanding the structure in depth — beyond just the auto-generated defaults — makes you a more effective developer and avoids a class of subtle dependency bugs.

attrs.alt: Annotated package.json showing identity fields, scripts, dependencies, devDependencies, and engines
attrs.title: Anatomy of package.json

content.text: The file is plain JSON — no comments allowed (use a linter like ESLint to catch common mistakes). The fields fall into four logical groups:

{
  "name": "my-react-app",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "description": "A React app built with Vite",
  "author": "Ada Lovelace <ada@example.com>",
  "license": "MIT"
}
— the package name used to import or reference it on the npm registry. For app projects that are never published, this is just an internal label.

After packages are installed (used for codegen, patching, Prisma generate)
This distinction determines what ends up in your production bundle and what stays in the development environment:
For front-end apps built with Vite, the distinction between
affect the production bundle. Vite's bundler decides what to include based on
statements, not where the package is listed. The classification still matters for readability, CI scripts that run
, and libraries you publish to npm.

# Add a runtime dependency:
npm install react-router-dom
# adds to "dependencies"

# Add a dev-only dependency:
npm install -D @types/react
# adds to "devDependencies"

# Install without devDependencies (production server):
npm install --omit=dev
— they specify a constraint, not a single version. The package manager resolves the latest version that satisfies the constraint and records the exact resolved version in the lock file.
) is the default prefix added by
. It allows minor and patch upgrades but not major (breaking) changes. The tilde (
) is more conservative — patch upgrades only.
How the lock file pins exact versions
) records the exact version installed for every package, including transitive dependencies. This means:
on a fresh clone installs exactly the locked versions, ignoring the ranges in
re-resolves within the ranges and writes new locked versions.
You can inspect the locked version with
— it shows exactly what is installed.
declare libraries that a package requires the
to provide — they are not installed automatically. This is how React component libraries work: they list
as a peer dependency so they share the app's React instance rather than bundling their own.
When you install such a library in your app, npm (v7+) auto-installs peer dependencies if they are not already present. If you have a conflicting version installed, npm will warn you.

are packages that are desirable but not required. If they fail to install (platform-incompatible native modules, for example), the package manager continues without error. The consuming code must check whether the package is available before using it.
Sometimes a transitive dependency (a dependency of a dependency) has a bug or security issue. You can force a specific version across your entire tree:
— documents the supported Node/npm version. With
, installing on an incompatible version fails immediately with a clear error instead of a cryptic later failure.
— used by corepack to enforce a specific package manager and version for the project. Set via
Tells bundlers which files have side effects (CSS, polyfills).
Defines the public API of a library — controls what paths are importable and provides ESM / CJS dual exports.
Defines workspace packages in a monorepo (e.g.,
Tells Vite, Babel, and PostCSS which browsers to target for transpilation and polyfills.

Editing package.json by hand without reinstalling
— manually changing a version number does not update
could jump to a breaking major release. Only the lock file saves you, and the lock file can be regenerated. Use explicit ranges.

Useful Commands for Working with package.json

# See what is installed and what version:
npm list --depth=0

# Check for outdated packages:
npm outdated

# Update a single package to latest within its range:
npm update react

# Update to a specific version (may exceed range):
npm install react@18.3.1

# Remove a package:
npm uninstall lodash

# Audit for known vulnerabilities:
npm audit
npm audit fix

# Check what a package resolves to in the lock file:
npm ls react
file) tells Vite, Babel, and PostCSS which browsers to support. The bundler uses this list to decide which JavaScript syntax to downcompile and which CSS prefixes to add.
A narrower browserslist means less transpilation and a smaller bundle — modern React apps targeting evergreen browsers can drop
Tool configuration can live in dedicated config files (
. For small projects, inline keeps everything in one place:

configuration works together with Husky git hooks to lint and format only the files you are actually committing — running ESLint over the entire codebase on every commit would be too slow. Install both:
Several tools help validate and analyse the health of your dependency configuration:

# Check for issues in package.json:
npm doctor

# Interactively update dependencies:
npx npm-check-updates -i

# Detect duplicate packages:
npm dedupe

# Find unused dependencies (install separately):
npx depcheck

# Estimate bundle size impact before installing:
npx bundlephobia-cli react@18
The exports Field for Library Authors
If you build a component library or utility package (not just an app), the
field controls exactly what consumers can import. It replaces the older
fields and supports conditional exports for ESM and CJS:

declaration allows tree-shaking tools to safely remove any exported symbol that is not imported — critical for keeping consumer bundles small. If your library imports CSS directly (a side effect), list those files:
Build tools, not shipped to users
, you are not just installing that one package. Its own
lists its own dependencies, which have dependencies of their own, and so on. These are called
and they make up the bulk of your

# You install:
npm install react-query

# node_modules ends up with:
# react-query (the package you asked for)
# @tanstack/query-core (react-query's dependency)
# ... and potentially dozens more

# Count how many packages you now have:
ls node_modules | wc -l

# See the full tree of who installed what:
npm ls --depth=3 react-query

The lock file records every transitive dependency and its exact version. This is why the lock file can be thousands of lines long even for a project with only 20 direct dependencies. Package managers resolve the transitive tree once, then reuse the locked result — subsequent installs are faster because no resolution step is needed.
— the scope groups related packages under an organisation or user namespace. Almost all TypeScript type definitions use the

Scoped packages on the npm public registry can be either public (free) or private (paid npm account). GitHub Packages, Artifactory, Verdaccio, and other registries support private scoped packages for teams that cannot publish to the public npm registry.
Practical: Adding Dependencies to a Vite + React Project
Walk through adding a few common packages to a freshly scaffolded Vite React app to see how

# 1. Scaffold the app:
npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts
cd my-app && npm install

# 2. Add routing (runtime):
npm install react-router-dom
# "react-router-dom": "^6.x.x" now in dependencies

# 3. Add TanStack Query for data fetching (runtime):
npm install @tanstack/react-query
# added to dependencies

# 4. Add Zod for schema validation (runtime):
npm install zod

# 5. Add testing utilities (dev only):
npm install -D @testing-library/react @testing-library/user-event jsdom
# added to devDependencies

# 6. Add Prettier (dev only):
npm install -D prettier

# Inspect the result:
cat package.json
